title: The Great Gatsby
description: In the summer of 1922, Nick Carraway moves to West Egg, Long Island, where he becomes drawn into the lavish world of his mysterious neighbor Jay Gatsby. As Gatsby's obsessive pursuit of Daisy Buchanan unravels, Nick witnesses the corruption of the American Dream — the hollowness of wealth, the impossibility of recapturing the past, and the careless destruction wrought by those who mistake money for meaning.

S-GBY-009.summary: On Sunday mornings, Gatsby's lawn becomes a vibrant, chaotic spectacle as the world and its mistress descend upon his mansion. S-GBY-034.summary: Months later, Nick encounters Tom Buchanan on Fifth Avenue, leading to a final, tense confrontation. Tom defiantly admits to telling Wilson that Gatsby owned the yellow car, justifying his actions by claiming Gatsby 'had it coming to him' and expressing his own 'suffering' over Myrtle's death. Nick, unable to forgive or like him, recognizes Tom and Daisy as 'careless people' who destroy lives and retreat into their wealth. He then has a final, strained conversation with Jordan Baker, where she accuses him of being a 'bad driver' in their relationship, before Nick, profoundly disillusioned, decides to return to the Midwest, reflecting on Gatsby's unattainable dream and the corrupting influence of the East.

worldSummary: Nick Carraway moves to West Egg, Long Island, to pursue a career in bonds, settling next to the mysterious Jay Gatsby. He visits his cousin Daisy and her husband Tom Buchanan in East Egg, where he is introduced to Jordan Baker and quickly becomes aware of the underlying tensions and moral decay within their opulent lives, including Tom's infidelity and Daisy's profound unhappiness. Nick is unwillingly drawn into Tom Buchanan's sordid affair when he is forced to accompany Tom and his mistress, Myrtle Wilson, to a raucous party in a New York apartment. Amidst the drunken revelry and superficiality, Nick observes the crude dynamics of their relationship and the moral decay of the city, culminating in Tom violently assaulting Myrtle. Nick attends one of Gatsby's extravagant parties, where he finally meets his mysterious host and observes the superficiality and moral ambiguity of the guests. Later, he reflects on his own life in New York and reveals Jordan Baker's dishonesty, which he paradoxically finds appealing, hinting at his own evolving moral compass. Nick recounts the endless parade of guests at Gatsby's parties, then Gatsby takes him on a bewildering drive to New York, revealing a fabricated past before producing proof of his war heroism and Oxford attendance. Gatsby then introduces Nick to Meyer Wolfshiem, a notorious gambler who fixed the 1919 World Series, further deepening the mystery of Gatsby's wealth. Later, Jordan Baker reveals Gatsby's true motive: he bought his mansion solely to be near Daisy and wants Nick to arrange a reunion, exposing the profound romantic longing beneath Gatsby's extravagant facade. Gatsby's long-awaited reunion with Daisy is fraught with awkwardness and intense emotion, revealing the depth of his obsession and the fragility of his carefully constructed world. Nick observes the profound impact Daisy has on Gatsby, as well as the subtle signs that Gatsby's idealized vision may be unsustainable. This chapter delves into Gatsby's true origins as James Gatz, revealing his self-invention and his formative years with Dan Cody, which shaped his ambition and wealth. Later, Tom and Daisy Buchanan attend one of Gatsby's parties, where Daisy is unimpressed by the West Egg crowd, leading to Gatsby's profound disappointment and his desperate desire to recreate the past. Gatsby's parties cease, revealing his singular focus on Daisy. A sweltering afternoon at the Buchanan's escalates into a tense confrontation in New York, where Tom exposes Gatsby's illicit past. The chapter culminates in a tragic car accident involving Myrtle Wilson, with Daisy at the wheel, and Gatsby's desperate attempt to protect her, further entangling their fates. After the accident, Nick confronts Gatsby, who reveals the full, tragic story of his past with Daisy, his carefully constructed persona, and his enduring, almost delusional, hope. Meanwhile, George Wilson, consumed by grief and suspicion, begins his relentless hunt for the yellow car and its driver, culminating in Gatsby's murder and Wilson's suicide. Following Gatsby's murder, Nick Carraway struggles to arrange a funeral, facing the indifference and betrayal of Gatsby's former associates and Daisy. Only Gatsby's estranged father and Owl Eyes attend, highlighting the superficiality of Gatsby's social world. Nick, disillusioned with the East, confronts Tom Buchanan, who reveals his role in Gatsby's death, before Nick ultimately decides to return to the Midwest, reflecting on Gatsby's unattainable dream and the moral decay of the wealthy.
